coredata.dc:title: Reconfigurable and nonvolatile bioresistive memory based on Scindapsus aureus leaves
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lu
Yang, Jing
Zhang, Yukai
Ju, Yuehang
Yang, Tianyu
Xie, Jiachu
Wen, Dianzhong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, Al/Scindapsus aureus (SA)/ITO and Al/polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA)/SA:graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/PMMA/ITO devices of different memory types and with different electrical characteristics were fabricated based on SA. Al/SA/ITO devices have bipolar resistance switching characteristics with a switching current ratio of 103 and a holding time of more than 104 s. When an Al/SA/ITO device is fabricated on a flexible substrate, the device still maintains stable resistance switching characteristics. After the addition of GQDs into the dielectric layer, the switching current ratio is increased (>105). Accordingly, Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO resistive memory was fabricated based on SA and GQD composites. Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass devices also show bipolar resistance switching characteristics; a single memory cell can be switched more than 200 times continuously, with the lowest threshold voltage, and the holding time is greater than 2 × 104 s. A cell of the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/PET memristors fabricated in this study can exhibit both bipolar and write-once-read-many-times (WORM) memory characteristics under different limited currents. The results show that SA is a promising biomaterial for memory fabrication, and this research opens up a new avenue for the application and development of memory with multiple functions and flexible wearable electronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: WORM memory
Scindapsus aureus leaves
Graphene quantum dots
Flexible device

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62371183 ), the Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. LH2021F045 ) and the Basic Research Project funded by the Basic Research Business Funding for Provincial Universities in Heilongjiang Province (Grant No. 2022-KYYWF-1088 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a)-b) Schematic diagram of the Al/SA/ITO device structure, c)-d) Schematic diagram of the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO device structure. SA thin film characterization: e) UV–vis absorption spectrum, f) FTIR spectrum and g) EDS spectrum. h) SEM images of the cross-section of the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO device.

Al/SA/ITO/glass device: a) I-V characteristic curve. b) Superimposed I-V characteristic curves. c) Switching current ratio and d) resistance retention time. Al/SA/ITO/PET device: e) I-V characteristic curves of the set process when applying a positive voltage and f) the set process when applying a negative voltage. (Insets: switching current ratios in the different voltage scanning directions.) Al/SA/ITO/glass device characteristics: g) cumulative probability curves for resistance values and h) Vset and Vreset distributions.

Al/SA:GQDs/ITO/glass device characteristics: a) I-V characteristic curve, b) I-V characteristic curves under continuous cyclic scanning of a single memory cell and c) I-V characteristics for the devices with different GQDs concentrations. Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass device characteristics: d) I-V characteristic curve, e) I-V characteristic curve under continuous cyclic scanning of a single memory cell and f) I-V characteristic curve of 50 cells. g) Switching current ratios of Al/SA/ITO/glass, Al/SA:GQDs/ITO/glass and Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass devices. Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass device characteristics: h) retention time and i) threshold voltage distributions, j) the influence of the Al electrode area on the I-V characteristics of the device.

I−V curves of the flexible Al/PMMA/SA:GQDS/PMMA/ITO/PET device: a) ICC= 100 mA in the negative voltage region and ICC= 1 mA in the positive voltage region and b) ICC= 1 mA at both positive and negative voltages. c) Diagram of device bending. d) Resistance state distribution of the device after bending.
Log(I)-log(V) curves with fitting curves for the Al/SA/ITO device: a) at negative voltages and b) at positive voltages. c) Diagram of the conductive mechanism of the Al/SA/ITO device.
Diagrams of the conductive mechanism of the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO device.
Electrical characteristics of memristors fabricated from S. aureus (SA) and other biomaterials.
Statistical results of mean and median values of VSET and VRESET for each device type.

head.ce:title: Reconfigurable and nonvolatile bioresistive memory based on Scindapsus aureus leaves

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic Engineering, Heilongjiang University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150080, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic Engineering, Heilongjiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronic Engineering, Heilongjiang University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150080, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, Al/Scindapsus aureus (SA)/ITO and Al/polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA)/SA:graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/PMMA/ITO devices of different memory types and with different electrical characteristics were fabricated based on SA. Al/SA/ITO devices have bipolar resistance switching characteristics with a switching current ratio of 103 and a holding time of more than 104 s. When an Al/SA/ITO device is fabricated on a flexible substrate, the device still maintains stable resistance switching characteristics. After the addition of GQDs into the dielectric layer, the switching current ratio is increased (>105). Accordingly, Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO resistive memory was fabricated based on SA and GQD composites. Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass devices also show bipolar resistance switching characteristics; a single memory cell can be switched more than 200 times continuously, with the lowest threshold voltage, and the holding time is greater than 2 × 104 s. A cell of the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/PET memristors fabricated in this study can exhibit both bipolar and write-once-read-many-times (WORM) memory characteristics under different limited currents. The results show that SA is a promising biomaterial for memory fabrication, and this research opens up a new avenue for the application and development of memory with multiple functions and flexible wearable electronic devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, magnetic memory, phase change memory, ferroelectric memory and resistive memory are widely studied [1–5]. Resistive random access memory (RRAM) has a faster read-write speed, a higher storage density and higher integration [6–8]. The reported dielectric layers used in memristors can be divided into organic materials [3,9,10], metal oxide materials [4,11], 2D materials [12–14], and perovskite-structured materials [5,15]. However, the excessive accumulation of harmful substances and electronic waste from these electronic devices cannot be ignored, and there is an urgent need to use biological materials to fabricate memory to reduce environmental pollution from electronic products. In addition, because biomaterials are abundant in nature, using them to make electronic products can further reduce costs [16–19]. Based on the unique properties and advantages of biomaterials, several reports have discussed the application of biomaterials as active layers in resistive memory [20–25]. As shown in Table 1, a transparent biomemristor was prepared by using natural silk protein [26], a memristor made of aloe exhibited bipolar resistance switching characteristics with a switching current ratio greater than 103 [27], a Pt/DNA:Cu2+/FTO memristor was fabricated based on DNA:Cu2+ composites [28], and a Ag/leaves/Ti/PET memristor was prepared by treating dead leaves as a dielectric layer [29]. Moreover, a Ag/orange peel/FTO device prepared by extracting pectin from orange peel achieved a switching current ratio of 103 [30]. The above biomaterials all show resistance switching behavior, but the switching current ratio of such devices is generally not high (∼103), and their stability also needs to be further improved. As shown in Table 1, compared to other reported biomemristors, the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass memristor proposed in this work exhibits a higher switching current ratio and longer holding times. Additionally, the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/PET memristor proposed in this work not only has a high current switching ratio but also demonstrates both bipolar and WORM switching behaviors.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are rich in functional groups controlling electron–hole recombination and have many advantages, such as good biocompatibility, low cost, and high chemical inertness [31,32]. Sokolov reported biocompatible electronic synapses based on nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (N-GOQDs), which exhibit threshold resistance switching characteristics [33]. Ali developed a memristor based on N-GOQDs and used UV light to regulate the electronic transition of the N-GOQDs to promote the formation of Ag conducting filaments from Ag+. This memristor can simulate synaptic functions induced by photoelectricity [34]. Yan introduced a graphene oxide quantum dot (GOQD) layer into Zr0.5Hf0.5O2 (ZHO) films to prepare Ag/ZHO/GOQDs/ZHO/Pt devices. Compared with devices without GOQDs, devices with GOQD layers have lower threshold voltages, more uniform threshold voltage distributions, and faster switching speeds [35].

As a natural biomaterial, the leaves of SA contain many terpene compounds. In this study, memristors with different structures were prepared by using SA as a dielectric layer, and the effects of doping with GQDs on the switching current ratios of the devices were investigated. To improve the device stability, Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO resistive memory was fabricated by using PMMA as the insulating layer and a composite of SA and GQDs as the active layer. A single memory cell can be continuously switched more than 200 times. The reported Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/PET resistive memory on a flexible substrate can exhibit two types of electrical characteristics, namely, bipolar and WORM memory characteristics, under different limited currents, thus establishing a new avenue for the design of biomemristors to achieve data storage diversity. A reconfigurable memristor is a special type of memristor whose resistance state can be reconfigured or adjusted by applying different voltages or currents. In addition, the device is capable of switching between different resistance states, enabling a variety of data storage and computing operations, making it flexible and programmable.

Fresh leaves of SA were cleaned with distilled water to remove dirt. The leaves were crushed evenly, and the supernatant was filtered with an injection filter to obtain the liquid. ITO/glass and ITO/PET (2 cm × 2 cm) were used as the bottom electrode and substrate materials, respectively. The substrates were placed in acetone, ethanol and deionized water in turn for ultrasonic cleaning for 30 min. The SA liquid was coated onto the ITO electrode at 1000 rpm for 20 s and dried at 80 ℃ for 1 h to obtain the dried active layers. Then, Al/SA/ITO/glass and Al/SA/ITO/PET devices were fabricated by forming an Al electrode on each dielectric layer via thermal evaporation at a pressure of 2 × 10−3 Pa. GQDs (30 wt%) were added to the SA liquid, and the mixture was ultrasonically oscillated for 10 min. PMMA powder was dissolved in anisole to obtain a PMMA solution with a concentration of 70 mg/ml. The PMMA solution was coated onto the cleaned substrates at a speed of 1000 rpm for 20 s and then vacuum dried at 80 ℃ for 1 h. Then, the mixed solution of SA and GQDs was coated onto the substrate ITO electrode at a speed of 1000 rpm for 20 s and then vacuum dried at 80 ℃ for 1 h. Then, a PMMA insulating layer was spin coated onto the dry dielectric layer using the same PMMA preparation process and then dried. Finally, Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass and Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/PET devices were fabricated by evaporating Al electrodes at 2 × 10−3 Pa. The area of the device (containing 20 cells) is 2 cm * 2 cm= 4 cm2, and the diameter of the Al electrode is 1 mm.

The UV–vis spectra of the SA films were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV/Vis, TU-1901) (Puxi, Beijing, China). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, 460ATR, Thermo 1410) (Nicolet, USA) was used to measure the functional groups in the films. Scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and energy dispersive spectrometry were used for elemental analysis of the samples. The electrical properties of the devices were measured using a semiconductor parameter tester (Keithley 4200).

The schematic structures of the Al/SA/ITO/glass device and the Al/SA/ITO/PET device are shown in Fig. 1a and b, respectively. Fig. 1c-d shows schematic diagrams of the structures of the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass and Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/PET devices, respectively. The UV–vis spectrum of the SA film is shown in Fig. 1e, and the absorption peak is at 493 nm. The band gap Eg of SA is calculated to be 2.51 eV from the formula
E
g
=
h c
/ λ
. To identify the functional groups of the compounds in SA, FTIR analysis of the SA film was carried out, as shown in Fig. 1f. Due to the stretching of the OH group, the sample showed a wide absorption band at 3456 cm−1, which is characteristic of carbohydrate monomers, including mannose and uronic acid. The absorption peak at 1637 cm−1 is related to the stretching of C-O and CO in SA, which indicates that this material contains a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups. We performed EDS energy spectrum analysis on the surface of the SA sample, as shown in Fig. 1g. The results show that SA contains N, Na, Mg and other metal elements in addition to C, O and K in the organic medium layer [36]. Fig. 1h shows SEM images of the cross-section of the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO device. The thicknesses of the layers from top to bottom are Al (46 nm), PMMA (13 nm), SA: GQDs (20 nm), PMMA (13 nm) and ITO (181 nm).

Fig. 2a shows the I-V characteristic curve of an Al/SA/ITO/glass device, which shows bipolar switching behavior. During the test, the ITO electrode was kept grounded, and a voltage scan was applied to the Al electrode. To avoid permanent dielectric layer breakdown, the compliance current was set to 0.1 A. The initial state of the device was the high-resistance state (HRS). First, a voltage scan of 5 V→0 V was applied to the device, and the device remained in the HRS. Subsequently, a voltage scan of 0 V→− 5 V was applied to the device; once the voltage had increased to − 0.80 V, the current flowing through the device suddenly increased, and the device switched from the HRS to the low-resistance state (LRS), corresponding to the set process (Vset = −0.80 V). When a − 5 V→0 V voltage scan was then applied to the device, the device remained in the LRS; when a further 0 V→5 V voltage scan was applied to the device, once the voltage had increased to 3.90 V, the device returned from the LRS to the HRS, corresponding to the reset process (Vreset = 3.90 V). The device can be continuously switched in this way more than 100 times on the same memory cell, as shown in Fig. 2b. These results show that the presented Al/SA/ITO/glass device has stable resistance switching memory characteristics.

The switching current ratio is a key parameter for the practical application of resistance switching memory. The higher the switching current ratio of a device is, the lower the probability of data misreading. Fig. 2c shows the switching current ratio of the presented Al/SA/ITO/glass device. The maximum switching current ratio of the device is 2.83 × 103, and the HRS and LRS can be maintained for 104 s without degradation, as shown in Fig. 2d.

The I-V characteristics of a flexible Al/SA/ITO/PET device were also tested. The compliance current was 10 mA in the positive voltage region, and the voltage scanning profile was 0 V→ 5 V→0 V→− 5 V→0 V, as shown in Fig. 2e. For the negative voltage region, we again set the compliance current to 10 mA, and the voltage scanning profile was 0 V→− 5 V →0 V→5 V→0 V, as shown in Fig. 2f. The results show that the Al/SA/ITO/PET device exhibits bipolar resistance switching behavior in both voltage scanning modes. The maximum switching current ratios of the device when set by applying a positive voltage and a negative voltage are 4.95 × 102 and 1.87 × 103, respectively, indicating that an Al/SA/ITO device on a flexible substrate can still maintain a large storage window.

To analyze the reliability of the device, we calculated the resistance of the high-resistance state (RHRS) and the resistance of the low-resistance state (RLRS) at 1 V from the I−V characteristic curve of a continuous voltage sweep performed for 100 cycles. Fig. 2g shows the cumulative probability curves for RHRS and RLRS. The median value of RHRS is 6.50 × 104 Ω, the median value of RLRS is 39.98 Ω, and the coefficients of variation are 1.78 and 0.06. The coefficient of variation of RLRS is smaller, and the distribution is more uniform. Fig. 2h shows the histograms of the Vset and Vreset distributions of the Al/SA/ITO/glass device during 100 consecutive voltage scanning cycles. The average value of Vset is − 1.66 V, the standard deviation is 0.63, and the coefficient of variation is 0.38; similarly, the average value of Vreset is 3.59 V, the standard deviation is 0.51, and the coefficient of variation is 0.14. The Vreset distribution of the device is more uniform.

To improve the electrical properties of the device, we introduced GQDs, which provide additional charge trapping capabilities, into the dielectric layer and prepared an Al/SA:GQDs/ITO/glass device. The I-V characteristic curve of the device is shown in Fig. 3a. A continuous voltage scanning test was conducted, and the corresponding superimposed I-V characteristic curves are shown in Fig. 3b. The performance of a single storage cell gradually degraded during this test, and the set voltage became unstable after 20 iterations of continuous resistance switching. Fig. 3(c) shows the device I-V characteristic curves of GQDs with doping concentrations of 0, 5 wt%, 15 wt% and 30 wt% in the active layer. The switching current ratio increases for the devices with increasing GQD concentration. When the concentration of GQDs is 30 wt%, the maximum switching current ratio of the device is 1.15 × 105, which can avoid the problem of misreading the switching state of the device due to the small switching current ratio. Therefore, 30 wt% GQD is selected for the device fabrication.

The Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass resistive memory device was prepared, and its I−V characteristic curve was measured, as shown in Fig. 3d. The I-V characteristic curve under continuous cyclic scanning of a single memory cell is shown in Fig. 3e. The device maintained stable resistance switching behavior for 200 switching cycles, indicating that the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass structure offers more stable device performance. The I-V characteristic curves of the 50 Al/PMMA/SA:GQD/PMMA/ITO/glass devices are shown in Fig. 3f, indicating good consistency among the devices. The superimposed I−V characteristic curves of a single memory cell of the device under multiple switching cycles are shown in Fig. 3f. The device maintained stable resistance switching behavior for 200 switching cycles, indicating that the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass structure offers more stable device performance.

The switching current ratios of the Al/SA/ITO/glass, Al/SA:GQDs/ITO/glass and Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass devices are shown in Fig. 3g. The maximum current switching ratios are 2.83 × 103, 1.15 × 105 and 1.66 × 105. Compared with that of the Al/SA/ITO/glass device, the current switching ratios of the two GQD-doped devices are significantly increased.

To explore the data maintenance performance of the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass device, the resistance values were read out under a bias voltage of 1 V, as shown in Fig. 3h. Both the HRS and LRS of the device were maintained for 2 × 104 s without significant degradation. Fig. 3i shows the threshold voltage distributions of the device. The Vset range is from − 1.30 V to − 0.10 V, with a mean value of − 0.66 V, a standard deviation of 0.22, and a coefficient of variation of 0.33. The Vreset range is from 1.90 V to 4.35 V, with a mean value of 3.05 V, a standard deviation of 0.58, and a coefficient of variation of 0.19. Compared with the Al/SA/ITO/glass device, the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass device has a lower operating voltage. As shown in Fig. 3j, there is no significant difference in the I-V characteristics of the devices with Al electrode diameters of 0.25 mm, 0.5 mm, and 1 mm.

Table 2 provides statistical results of the mean and median of VSET and VRESET for Al/SA/ITO/glass, Al/SA:GQD/ITO/glass, and Al/PMMA/SA:GQD/PMMA/ITO/glass devices. It is evident that compared to Al/SA/ITO/glass devices, those (Al/SA:GQD/ITO/glass) doped with GQDs have increased Vset and decreased Vreset. Furthermore, the introduction of the PMMA layer in Al/PMMA/SA:GQD/PMMA/ITO/glass devices results in the lowest Vset and Vreset.

We tested the electrical properties of a flexible Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/PET biological memristor. First, the behavior of the switching resistance under different compliance currents will be discussed, as shown in Fig. 4a. ICC= 1 mA was set in the positive voltage region, ICC= 100 mA was set in the negative voltage region, and the voltage scanning profile was 0 V→5 V→0 V→− 5 V→0 V. In the positive voltage region, once the voltage had increased to 3.50 V, the current of the device suddenly increased, and the device switched from the HRS to the LRS. In the negative voltage region, once the voltage had increased to − 1.45 V, the current of the device suddenly decreased, and the device recovered to the HRS. Under continuous voltage scanning, the device could switch between the high- and low-resistance states, thus proving that the device has the "write-read-erase-read" function [37–39]. For the measurements shown in Fig. 4b, a compliance current of 1 mA was set in both the positive and negative voltage regions. When a positive voltage of 1.20 V was applied, the current suddenly increased, and the device switched to the LRS. During the subsequent positive and negative bias scanning, the device remained switched on. Hence, the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/PET device does not undergo an erasure process in the negative voltage region, showing WORM storage behavior. This device exhibits two different types of electrical characteristics under different compliance currents. To test the flexibility of the device, the electrical characteristics of the device were tested in the bent state (bending the 20 mm * 20 mm device to 20 mm * 16 mm), as shown in Fig. 4c. In addition, to test the stability after bending, we tested the high- and low-resistance state distributions of the device after 104 bending cycles, as shown in Fig. 4d.

To study the resistance switching behavior of the Al/SA/ITO device, the positive and negative voltage regions of the I-V characteristic curve were fitted in double logarithmic coordinates. As shown in Fig. 5a, in the negative voltage region, the slope of the fitted curve in the LRS is 1.01, which confirms the ohmic conducting behavior. However, in the HRS state, the I-V curve is obviously divided into two parts. The fitted slope of the device at low voltages (0∼−0.39 V) is 1.45, showing ohmic behavior, whereas the slope of the curve increases rapidly to 2.93 at high voltages (−0.39 V∼−1.48 V), consistent with the space charge limiting current (SCLC) effect. This is because there are some traps in the device, and the injected charge carriers become trapped in the defects [39]. When the voltage exceeds − 1.48 V, all defects are filled, the device is set, and the state switches from the HRS to the LRS. The charge conduction mechanism in the positive voltage region is similar to that in the negative voltage region, as shown in Fig. 5b.

Memristors with conductive filaments formed from metal ions have been widely reported [40,41]. For the Al/SA/ITO device, metal ions and oxygen vacancies in the SA are the main causes of resistance switching. When a larger compliance current (Icc=100 mA) is set in the negative direction and a voltage exceeding Vset is applied to the top electrode, due to the significant electric field, negatively charged oxygen ions in the SA layer drift toward the top electrode, while metal ions move toward the bottom electrode. In this process, the molecular chains in the pothos leaf provide a pathway for these ions to move. Oxidized metal ions are reduced to form conductive filaments, switching the device from the HRS to the LRS. Conversely, when a voltage exceeding Vreset is applied to the top electrode, the opposite process occurs. The conductive filaments break, and the device returns to the HRS, exhibiting bipolar resistive switching characteristics. When a smaller compliance current (Icc=1 mA) is set in the negative direction, the external energy applied after the formation of the conductive filaments is insufficient to break them. In other words, the device remains in the LRS after it is set up, so the device exhibits WORM behavior.

According to the energy band diagram, the conduction mechanism of the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass device can be explained as shown in Fig. 6. The work functions of ITO and aluminum are 4.6 eV [42] and 4.3 eV [43], respectively. The bandgaps of PMMA, SA, and GQDs are 4.2 eV [44], 2.5 eV, and 2.4 eV [45], respectively. The PMMA separates the SA:GQDs layer from the electrode and acts as a tunneling layer. GQDs have a charge trapping effect, and the addition of GQDs increases the energy band barrier. The defects in the SA form charge trapping centers, and the SA:GQD layer acts as a charge trapping layer, which further deepens the electron trapping depth of the composite film and increases the initial resistance of the device, thereby increasing the switching current ratio of the device and improving the device characteristics.

Fig. 6a shows the band diagram of the device at zero bias. As shown in Fig. 6b, when a negative voltage is applied to the Al electrode, the electrons directly tunnel into the SA:GQD layer. With an increase in the applied voltage, the energy band bends into a triangular barrier [41]. As shown in Fig. 6c, once the voltage increases to Vset, the trap states in the dielectric layer are filled, and the resistance state of the device suddenly changes from the HRS to the LRS. As shown in Fig. 6d, at this time, the transport of free electrons is allowed in the device, and the transferred electrons are constantly being released from the captured state. Therefore, the conduction mechanism of the LRS can be explained as ohmic conduction. When the trap states of the dielectric layer are filled with injected electrons, the device remains in the LRS even as a gradually decreasing negative voltage is applied. In contrast, as shown in Fig. 6e, when a positive voltage is applied to the Al electrode, the electrons in the dielectric layer are continuously released from the trap centers. When the voltage increases to Vreset, the conductive path is destroyed, and the device switches from the LRS to the HRS.

The Al/SA/ITO devices are prepared using the biomaterial SA as the dielectric layer, and the electrical properties of the devices are tested. It exhibits bipolar resistance switching characteristics. A memory cell can be continuously switched more than 100 times, the maximum current switching ratio can reach 2.83 × 103, and the retention time is longer than 104 s. With the introduction of GQDs into the dielectric layer, the current on-off ratio of the device increases; one storage cell of an Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/glass biomemristor can be switched more than 200 times, and the retention time is longer than 2 × 104 s. A flexible Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/PET device exhibits two types of electrical characteristics, namely, bipolar switching and WORM characteristics, under different compliance currents and scanning voltages. This article proves that SA is a high-quality active layer material for biomemristors. Devices made with SA have the advantages of a large switching ratio, environmental friendliness and sustainable development. This study provides new ideas for research on biological memristors and flexible electronic devices.

Flexible resistive memory with different storage properties is fabricated based on the biomaterial Scindapsus aureus(SA). By doping with graphene quantum dots(GQDs), the switching current ratio is increased, and the data misreading rate is reduced. A Al/polymethyl methacrylate(PMMA)/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/Glass device has more stable resistance switching behavior and a reduced set voltage, enabling low-power storage. A single storage cell of the Al/PMMA/SA:GQDs/PMMA/ITO/PET memristors fabricated in this study can exhibit both flash and WORM memory characteristics under different limited currents. The results show that SA is a promising biomaterial for memory fabrication, and this research opens up a new avenue for the application and development of memory with multiple functions and flexible wearable electronic devices. The biomemristors studied in this paper have the advantages of not only a simple structure, a small size, low power consumption, easy integration, and compatibility with CMOS processes but also high integrability with nonvolatile storage, artificial intelligence, image processing, gate circuits and logic operations, artificial neural networks and other promising applications.

Lu Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jing Yang: Investigation, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Yukai Zhang: Investigation, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Yuehang Ju: Investigation, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Tianyu Yang: Conceptualization. Jiachu Xie: Conceptualization. Dianzhong Wen: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62371183), the Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. LH2021F045) and the Basic Research Project funded by the Basic Research Business Funding for Provincial Universities in Heilongjiang Province (Grant No. 2022-KYYWF-1088).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The pH-controlled memristive effect in a sustainable bioelectronic device prepared using lotus root
Mater. Today Sustain.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Li, Y. Xu, M. Lei, Y. Zhao, B. Sun, H. Elshekh, L. Zheng, X. Zhang, W. Hou, Y. ACS Nano.11 (9) (2017), 9176–9182. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.7b04070

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lu Wang received a B.S. in microelectronic technology, an M.S. degree, and a Ph.D. in microelectronics and solid-state electronics from Heilongjiang University, Harbin, China, in 2001, 2004, and 2010, respectively. She is a Professor and master's supervisor at Heilongjiang University. She is currently the director of the Microelectronics Laboratory of Heilongjiang University. Her current research interests include volatile and nonvolatile memory, emerging memory devices, memristor logics, nanodevices, and neuromorphic computing.

Jing Yang has received her M.S. degree, and she is a graduate of the Microelectronics and Solid State Electronics at Heilongjiang University. Her research interests include memristors and integrated circuits.

Yukai Zhang has received his M.S. degree, and he is a graduate of the Microelectronics and Solid State Electronics at Heilongjiang University. His research interests include memristors and integrated circuits.
Yuehang Ju is currently working toward a M.S. in microelectronics and solid-state electronics in Heilongjiang University, Harbin, China. His research interests include memristors.

Tianyu Yang has received her M.S. degree, and she is a graduate of the Microelectronics and Solid State Electronics at Heilongjiang University. Her research interests include memristors and integrated circuits.

Jiachu Xie has received his M.S. degree and he is a graduate of the Integrated Circuit Engineering program at Heilongjiang University. His research interests include memristors and integrated circuits.
Dianzhong Wen is a professor and a doctoral supervisor at Heilongjiang University. His current research interests include emerging memory devices and neuromorphic computing.
